text: NIHSS is not just a score — it's a roadmap. Systematically walk through it: consciousness, gaze, visual fields, facial palsy, arm/leg motor, limb ataxia, sensory, language, dysarthria, neglect. It tells you exactly where the lesion is.

Time is brain. Approximately 1.9 million neurons die every minute during an ischaemic stroke. The mantra 'treat fast' has genuine biological basis — the faster the reperfusion, the greater the salvage of penumbral tissue.

Posterior circulation stroke is the great mimic. Vertigo, vomiting, and ataxia can be dismissed as labyrinthitis. HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) outperforms early MRI in detecting posterior stroke. A normal head impulse test in an acutely vertiginous patient = posterior fossa lesion until proven otherwise.

When asked about the future of stroke care, discuss the expansion of mechanical thrombectomy to 24-hour windows (DAWN, DEFUSE 3 trials) and the role of AI in automated perfusion imaging (RapidAI) to select patients beyond conventional time windows.

Secondary prevention is where neurology meets general medicine. Rate- vs rhythm-control in AF, dual antiplatelet in minor stroke/TIA (POINT trial), and statin intensity matter enormously — knowing this signals clinical depth.

Blood biomarkers in stroke are an active frontier: GFAP and UCH-L1 can distinguish haemorrhagic from ischaemic stroke within minutes. SERUM-biomarker-guided triage may one day reduce unnecessary CT head delays.

Audit idea: Door-to-needle times for thrombolysis at your trust vs national benchmark (60 min). Even small QIPs on checklist-driven protocols for ED stroke activation have strong publication potential.

Clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) — a first demyelinating episode — does not equal MS. MS diagnosis requires dissemination in time AND space (McDonald Criteria 2017). One T2 lesion with simultaneous gadolinium enhancement counts as both.

Uhthoff's phenomenon: transient neurological worsening with heat or exercise. This is NOT a relapse. Telling a patient this distinction avoids unnecessary MRI and steroid courses. It occurs because heat slows axonal conduction velocity in demyelinated fibres.
When assessing an MS patient, always ask about fatigue, bladder dysfunction, sexual dysfunction, and cognitive fog — these are often the most disabling symptoms and are frequently under-reported.

The MS treatment paradigm has shifted from 'escalation' to 'treat to target' — some centres now argue for early high-efficacy therapy (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab) rather than starting with platform therapies, especially in young patients with high lesion burden.

Serum neurofilament light chain (sNfL) is emerging as a sensitive biomarker of neuroaxonal injury. Elevated sNfL correlates with relapse activity, disability progression, and response to DMTs — potentially enabling monitoring without annual MRI.

The cardinal features are TRAP: Tremor (rest, pill-rolling, 4-6Hz), Rigidity (cogwheel), Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability. But bradykinesia is the sine qua non — you cannot diagnose PD without it.

Red flags for Parkinson's Plus syndromes: early falls (PSP), early autonomic failure (MSA), early dementia (DLB or MSA), symmetrical onset, poor levodopa response, cerebellar features. These should trigger rethinking the diagnosis.

Levodopa timing matters on the ward. Missed doses cause potentially life-threatening 'off' periods with immobility, dysphagia, and aspiration. Never stop Parkinson's medications perioperatively without specialist input.

Discuss disease-modification vs symptomatic treatment — currently all approved PD therapies are symptomatic. Alpha-synuclein-targeted therapies (e.g. Prasinezumab) are in Phase 2/3 trials and represent the frontier of PD research.

GBA1 mutations are the most common genetic risk factor for Parkinson's disease, conferring a 5–10× lifetime risk. This has opened the door to genotype-stratified trials and potential lysosomal pathway therapies.

Not all seizures are epilepsy. ILAE definition requires either 2+ unprovoked seizures ≥24h apart, OR one seizure with ≥60% recurrence risk. A provoked seizure (hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, alcohol withdrawal) does not = epilepsy.

PNES (Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Seizures) affects ~20% of patients referred to epilepsy services and is frequently misdiagnosed and over-treated. Features suggesting PNES: eyes closed during event, prolonged duration >2 min, ictal crying, preserved awareness during bilateral motor activity.

Status epilepticus management: the 30-minute rule. Lorazepam → repeat lorazepam → IV levetiracetam or sodium valproate → anaesthesia. Know this sequence and know the doses. On call neurologists are called for status — having this fluent impresses.

The epilepsy surgical revolution: SEEG (stereoelectroencephalography), robotic-assisted placement, and increasingly precise 3T MRI/PET co-registration mean more patients with drug-resistant epilepsy are candidates for curative surgery. This is a compelling area of growth in UK neurology.

SCN1A mutations (Dravet syndrome) are the prototypic genetic epilepsy. Sodium channel blockers (carbamazepine, lamotrigine) are contraindicated and worsen seizures — a critical clinical-genomics crossover that matters on the wards and in interviews.

Alzheimer's ≠ just memory loss. Posterior cortical atrophy causes visuospatial failure; logopenic aphasia variant affects language; frontal variant presents as behavioural change. Knowing the variants makes you sound far beyond the average IMT doctor.

Reversible causes of cognitive decline: B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, NPH (wet/wobbly/wacky triad), subdural haematoma, HIV, syphilis, Wernicke's. These should be excluded before any dementia diagnosis is accepted.

DLB vs Alzheimer's: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism are the core DLB features. Critical point: these patients are exquisitely sensitive to antipsychotics — can cause severe, irreversible NMS-like reactions. Never prescribe haloperidol in DLB.

The Lecanemab and Donanemab approval era is transforming Alzheimer's care. These anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies are the first DMTs shown to slow cognitive decline in early AD. Key concerns: ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities), patient selection (early disease, APOE4 genotype), and NHS accessibility.

Blood-based biomarkers for AD: plasma p-tau217, p-tau181, and Abeta42/40 ratio can now detect AD pathology with diagnostic accuracy approaching CSF. This is set to transform screening, clinical trial recruitment, and potentially diagnostic pathways in 5–10 years.

Thunderclap headache = subarachnoid haemorrhage until proven otherwise. Non-contrast CT head within 6h has ~98% sensitivity. If CT negative and suspicion remains: LP at 12h post-onset looking for xanthochromia.

Medication overuse headache (MOH) affects ~1–2% of the population and is the most underrecognised cause of chronic daily headache. Simple analgesics, triptans, or opioids used >10–15 days/month for 3+ months. Withdrawal is the treatment — warn patients headache initially worsens.

CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) are a genuine paradigm shift in migraine prevention — the first migraine-specific preventatives. Knowing the mechanism (blocking CGRP or its receptor at the trigeminal level) signals research-level understanding.

Relative Afferent Pupillary Defect (RAPD) — the swinging torch test — is one of the most important signs in neurology. RAPD = optic nerve or severe retinal disease. No RAPD in a 'blind' eye suggests non-organic visual loss.

CN III palsy algorithm: complete ptosis + down-and-out eye + dilated pupil = posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proven otherwise. This is a neurosurgical emergency. Pupil-sparing CN III palsy is more likely microvascular (diabetes/hypertension).

Foot drop is peroneal nerve until proven otherwise — but always exclude L4/L5 radiculopathy (hip abductor weakness absent in peroneal palsy, present in radiculopathy) and sciatic nerve lesion (hamstrings affected).

When asked 'why neurology?': frame it around diagnostic reasoning, the growing therapeutic landscape (the nihilism era is over), and the intersection of basic science and clinical medicine. Avoid generic answers — reference specific cases, podcasts, or experiences.

The cerebellar speech triad: dysarthria (slurred), dysmetria (past pointing), dysdiadochokinesia (impaired rapid alternating movements). DANISH — Dysdiadochokinesia, Ataxia, Nystagmus, Intention tremor, Slurred speech, Hypotonia.
